chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Discrete Structures for Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Mathematical foundations from discrete mathematics for analyzing computer algorithms, for both correctness and performance; introduction to models of computation, including finite state machines and Turing machines. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147, MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross-listed with ECEN 222.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147, MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross Listing: ECEN 222/CSCE 222.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the student who has done the requisite work will be able to:
• understand the basic principles of logic, proofs and sets.
• apply results from discrete mathematics to the analysis of algorithms.
• produce proofs by induction and apply counting techniques.
• explain models of computation.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• The grading scale is:
• A 90-100
• B 80-89
• C 70-79
• D 60-69
• F 59 or below
• Grades will be based on:
○35%: Final examination
• 25%: Midterm exam I
• 25%: Midterm exam II
○15% : Homework (about 6, weightings will vary but only slightly)

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• The due dates for the homework will always be at least 7 days after the homework is posted, and will be designated via canvas. Occasionally the instructor will permit a moderate extension to the deadlines, those extensions will apply to every student equally, and will be communicated in class and by updating the details in the canvas submission system.
• The course has a late policy with a discount of 33½ % per day. This is computed by the minute (not as a step-function at midnight) which works out to follow this rule (where t is in units of minutes late):
AwardedScore = max(0,(4320 - t)/4320)· RawScoreAsIfNotLate
• The discounting is linear and reaches zero after 3 days.
• Proof: 60 minutes in an hour, 24 hours in a day, so 3 days means 60×24×3 = 4320.
Of course 333 ×3 = 1.
• The midterm and final exams, being examinations, are not permitted to be late; they will be in-person written examinations. Midterms will be taken during class.
• Standard university reasons for lateness shall, of course, be respected so long as the student communicates with the instructor as soon as possible—this means, specifically, it has been communicated before the deadline has passed and not ex post facto whenever feasible. (Note that job interviews and attendance of the career fair are, as per Student Rule 7, generally not excused absences: but the instructor will judge them in his own discretion.)

chunks.title: Homework assignments
chunks.content: The only submissions for this course will be homeworks, these are to be completed in LaTeX and submitted electronically via canvas. There will be approximately six of these posted, thus, at a rate of about one every two weeks. They involve a substantial effort, despite only counting for 15% of the grade. They are intended as the exercises used to ensure you understand the material and prepare for the examinations. We do have a pair of undergraduate TAs who will help with the grading, however they will check only a subset of the questions for correctness, and assess the submission for completeness. Solutions will be posted ±3 days after the deadline.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Use of online resources, AI-tools, friends, etc. for your homework assignments
The homeworks are intended to give you cause to dig into the textbook, to think about the material (including pondering weird edge-cases) and gain facility with doing the mathematics. You may find other resources (e.g., online forums, your friends and classmates, your TAs, AI-tools) helpful. Any of these may be used, so long as you acknowledge this fact in your submissions. Attempt the homework, but use other materials once you get stuck: the more stubbornly you try things yourself, the better for development of your own understanding (within reason, because you are using time that displaces other learning experiences). The portion of the grade for the homeworks is really to provide enough incentive to do the homework assignments without them being "starved" by the other demands of your life.

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content:
Office Hours
Both the instructor and the course TA(s) office weekly office hours. You are strongly encouraged to attend these in order to obtain additional guidance and help.
Peer Teachers
The Peer Teacher (PT) program (Visit the Peer Teacher Central website) is designed to support your learning by bridging the gap between students and instructors. Peer Teachers are high-performing undergraduates who have successfully completed this course (or similar ones) and are eager to help others succeed.
PTs can help you:
• Understand course and lab material
• Practice computational thinking
• Improve your programming skills
Because they recently completed the same classes, PTs are in an excellent position to guide you through challenging concepts and assignments. Students consistently report that working with PTs has a positive impact on their performance. The program supports CSCE courses at the 100, 200, and 300 levels.
